href="#cite_note-337"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>335<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-338" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-338"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>336<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The event has been described as an attempted <a href="/wiki/Self-coup" title="Self-coup">self-coup</a> by Trump.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEPion-BerlinBruneauGoetze2022_339-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPion-BerlinBruneauGoetze2022-339"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>337<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEHarvey20223_340-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarvey20223-340"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>338<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECastañedaJenks2023246_341-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastañedaJenks2023246-341"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>339<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Congress later reconvened and confirmed Biden's victory in the early hours of January 7.<sup id="cite_ref-342" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-342"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>340<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Between_presidencies_(2021–2025)"><span id="Between_presidencies_.282021.E2.80.932025.29"></span>Between presidencies (2021–2025)</h2></div>
<p>Upon leaving the White House, Trump began living at Mar-a-Lago, establishing an office there as provided for by the <a href="/wiki/Former_Presidents_Act" title="Former Presidents Act">Former Presidents Act</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-343" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-343"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>341<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His continuing <a href="/wiki/Big_lie#Donald_Trump's_false_claims_of_a_stolen_election" title="Big lie">false claims concerning the 2020 election</a> were commonly referred to as the "<a href="/wiki/Big_lie" title="Big lie">big lie</a>" by his critics, although in May 2021, he and many of his supporters began using the term to refer to the election itself.<sup id="cite_ref-344" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-344"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>342<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-key2_345-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-key2-345"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>343<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The Republican Party used his false claims about the election to justify <a href="/wiki/Republican_efforts_to_restrict_voting_following_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election" title="Republican efforts to restrict voting following the 2020 United States presidential election">imposing new voting restrictions</a> in its favor.<sup id="cite_ref-346" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-346"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>344<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-347" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-347"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>345<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-348" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-348"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>346<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He continued to pressure state legislators to overturn the election.<sup id="cite_ref-349" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-349"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>347<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Unlike other former presidents, Trump continued to dominate his party; a 2022 profile in <i>The New York Times</i> described him as a modern <a href="/wiki/Party_boss" class="mw-redirect" title="Party boss">party boss</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-PB2_350-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PB2-350"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>348<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He continued fundraising, raising a <a href="/wiki/War_chest" title="War chest">war chest</a> containing more than twice that of the Republican Party, and profited from fundraisers many Republican candidates held at Mar-a-Lago. Much of his focus was on party governance and installing in key posts officials loyal to him.<sup id="cite_ref-PB2_350-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PB2-350"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>348<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/2022_midterm_elections" class="mw-redirect" title="2022 midterm elections">2022 midterm elections</a>, Trump endorsed over 200 candidates for various offices.<sup id="cite_ref-351" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-351"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>349<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In February 2022, his <a href="/wiki/Trump_Media_%26_Technology_Group" title="Trump Media & Technology Group">Trump Media & Technology Group</a> (TMTG) launched a pro-Trump social media platform called <a href="/wiki/Truth_Social" title="Truth Social">Truth Social</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-352" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-352"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>350<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In March 2024, TMTG became a <a href="/wiki/Public_company" title="Public company">public company</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-353" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-353"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>351<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Legal_issues">Legal issues</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Personal_and_business_legal_affairs_of_Donald_Trump" title="Personal and business legal affairs of Donald Trump">Personal and business legal affairs of Donald Trump</a> and <a href="/wiki/Legal_affairs_of_the_first_Trump_presidency" title="Legal affairs of the first Trump presidency">Legal affairs of the first Trump presidency</a></div>
<p>In 2019, journalist <a href="/wiki/E._Jean_Carroll" title="E. Jean Carroll">E. Jean Carroll</a> accused Trump of raping her in the 1990s and <a href="/wiki/E._Jean_Carroll_v._Donald_J._Trump" title="E. Jean Carroll v. Donald J. Trump">sued him for defamation</a> over his denial.<sup id="cite_ref-354" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-354"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>352<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Carroll sued him again in 2022 for <a href="/wiki/Battery_(crime)" title="Battery (crime)">battery</a> and further defamation.<sup id="cite_ref-355" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-355"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>353<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He was <a href="/wiki/Legal_liability" title="Legal liability">found liable</a> for sexual abuse and defamation and ordered to pay $5 million in one case<sup id="cite_ref-356" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-356"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>354<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and $83.3 million in the other.<sup id="cite_ref-uphold_357-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-uphold-357"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>355<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Federal appeals courts upheld both findings and awards in December 2024 and September 2025, respectively.<sup id="cite_ref-358" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-358"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>356<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-uphold_357-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-uphold-357"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>355<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2022, <a href="/wiki/New_York_business_fraud_lawsuit_against_the_Trump_Organization" title="New York business fraud lawsuit against the Trump Organization">New York filed a civil lawsuit</a> against Trump accusing him of inflating the Trump Organization's value to gain an advantage with lenders and banks.<sup id="cite_ref-359" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-359"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>357<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-TF_360-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-TF-360"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>358<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He was found liable and ordered to pay nearly $355 million plus interest.<sup id="cite_ref-TF_360-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-TF-360"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>358<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In August 2025, the appeals court upheld his liability and nonmonetary penalties, but voided the monetary penalty as excessive.<sup id="cite_ref-361" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-361"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>359<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In connection with Trump's efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his involvement in the January 6 attack, in December 2022 the <a href="/wiki/United_States_House_Select_Committee_on_the_January_6_Attack" class="mw-redirect" title="United States House Select Committee on the January 6 Attack">U.S. House committee on the attack</a> recommended criminal charges against him for <a href="/wiki/Obstructing_an_official_proceeding" title="Obstructing an official proceeding">obstructing an official proceeding</a>, inciting or assisting an insurrection, and <a href="/wiki/Conspiracy_to_defraud_the_United_States" class="mw-redirect" title="Conspiracy to defraud the United States">conspiracy to defraud the United States</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-362" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-362"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>360<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In August 2023, a grand jury in <a href="/wiki/Fulton_County,_Georgia" title="Fulton County, Georgia">Fulton County, Georgia</a>, indicted him on <a href="/wiki/Georgia_election_racketeering_prosecution" title="Georgia election racketeering prosecution">13 charges, including racketeering</a>, for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state.<sup id="cite_ref-363" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-363"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>361<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-364" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-364"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>362<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The case was dismissed in November 2025 after the new prosecutor declined to pursue the charges.<sup id="cite_ref-365" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-365"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>363<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg/250px-Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg/500px-Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3717" data-file-height="2473"></a><figcaption>Classified intelligence material found during search of Mar-a-Lago</figcaption></figure>
<p>In January 2022, the <a href="/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration" title="National Archives and Records Administration">National Archives and Records Administration</a> retrieved <a href="/wiki/Federal_prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_(classified_documents_case)" title="Federal prosecution of Donald Trump (classified documents case)">15 boxes of documents</a> Trump had taken to <a href="/wiki/Mar-a-Lago" title="Mar-a-Lago">Mar-a-Lago</a> after leaving the White House, some of which were classified.<sup id="cite_ref-cnn-tl222_366-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cnn-tl222-366"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>364<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In the ensuing Justice Department investigation, officials retrieved more classified documents from his lawyers.<sup id="cite_ref-cnn-tl222_366-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cnn-tl222-366"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>364<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> On August 8, 2022, <a href="/wiki/FBI_search_of_Mar-a-Lago" title="FBI search of Mar-a-Lago">FBI agents searched Mar-a-Lago</a> for illegally held documents, including those in breach of the <a href="/wiki/Espionage_Act" class="mw-redirect" title="Espionage Act">Espionage Act</a>, collecting 11 sets of classified documents, some marked top secret.<sup id="cite_ref-bddj0812222_367-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bddj0812222-367"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>365<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-NYT-20220812222_368-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20220812222-368"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>366<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A <a href="/wiki/Federal_grand_jury" class="mw-redirect" title="Federal grand jury">federal grand jury</a> constituted by Special Counsel <a href="/wiki/Jack_Smith_(lawyer)" title="Jack Smith (lawyer)">Jack Smith</a> indicted Trump in June 2023 on 31 counts of "willfully retaining national defense information" under the Espionage Act, among other charges.<sup id="cite_ref-cnn-tl222_366-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cnn-tl222-366"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>364<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-369" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-369"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>367<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-370" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-370"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>368<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump pleaded not guilty.<sup id="cite_ref-371" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-371"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>369<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In July 2024, judge <a href="/wiki/Aileen_Cannon" title="Aileen Cannon">Aileen Cannon</a> dismissed the case, ruling Smith's appointment as special prosecutor was unconstitutional.<sup id="cite_ref-CD2_372-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CD2-372"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>370<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> After his reelection, the <a href="/wiki/Federal_prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_(election_obstruction_case)" title="Federal prosecution of Donald Trump (election obstruction case)">2020 election obstruction case</a> and the classified documents case were dismissed <a href="/wiki/Prejudice_(legal_term)#With_prejudice_and_without_prejudice" title="Prejudice (legal term)">without prejudice</a> due to Justice Department policy against prosecuting sitting presidents.<sup id="cite_ref-dismiss2_373-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dismiss2-373"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>371<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In May 2024, Trump <a href="/wiki/Prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_in_New_York" title="Prosecution of Donald Trump in New York">was convicted on 34 felony counts</a> of <a href="/wiki/Falsifying_business_records" title="Falsifying business records">falsifying business records</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-conv_374-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-conv-374"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>372<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The case stemmed from evidence that he booked <a href="/wiki/Michael_Cohen_(lawyer)" title="Michael Cohen (lawyer)">Michael Cohen</a>'s hush-money payments to adult film actress <a href="/wiki/Stormy_Daniels" title="Stormy Daniels">Stormy Daniels</a> as business expenses to cover up his alleged 2006–2007 affair with Daniels during the 2016 election.<sup id="cite_ref-conv_374-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-conv-374"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>372<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-375" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-375"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>373<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> On January 10, 2025, the judge gave Trump a no-penalty sentence known as an unconditional discharge, saying that punitive requirements would have interfered with presidential immunity.<sup id="cite_ref-uds_376-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-uds-376"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>374<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="2024_presidential_election">2024 presidential election</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election" title="2024 United States presidential election">2024 United States presidential election</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_2024_presidential_campaign" title="Donald Trump 2024 presidential campaign">Donald Trump 2024 presidential campaign</a>, <a href="/wiki/2024_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries" title="2024 Republican Party presidential primaries">2024 Republican Party presidential primaries</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Second_presidential_transition_of_Donald_Trump" title="Second presidential transition of Donald Trump">Second presidential transition of Donald Trump</a></div>
<p>In November 2022, Trump announced his candidacy for the 2024 presidential election and created a fundraising account.<sup id="cite_ref-377" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-377"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>375<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-378" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-378"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>376<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In March 2023, the campaign began diverting ten percent of the donations to his <a href="/wiki/Leadership_PAC" class="mw-redirect" title="Leadership PAC">leadership PAC</a>. His campaign had paid $100 million towards his legal bills by March 2024.<sup id="cite_ref-379" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-379"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>377<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-380" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-380"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>378<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump's policy platform, named <a href="/wiki/Agenda_47" title="Agenda 47">Agenda 47</a>, was vague and aimed at Republican primaries.<sup id="cite_ref-381" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-381"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>379<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He occasionally disavowed knowledge of <a href="/wiki/Project_2025" title="Project 2025">Project 2025</a>, an outline for consolidating power in the executive branch,<sup id="cite_ref-382" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-382"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>380<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> despite personnel overlap with his first administration.<sup id="cite_ref-383" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-383"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>381<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-384" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-384"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>382<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> During the campaign, he made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements.<sup id="cite_ref-385" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-385"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>383<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
He said that he would weaponize the FBI and the Justice Department against his political opponents<sup id="cite_ref-386" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-386"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>384<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-387" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-387"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>385<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and use the military to target Democratic politicians and those not supporting his candidacy.<sup id="cite_ref-388" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-388"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>386<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Lerer_10152024_389-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lerer_10152024-389"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>387<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He used harsher and more dehumanizing anti-immigrant rhetoric than during his presidency.<sup id="cite_ref-390" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-390"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>388<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
His rhetoric, calling his political opponents "the enemy", vermin, and fascists, has been described by some historians and scholars as authoritarian, <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_fascism" title="Donald Trump and fascism">fascist</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-391" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-391"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
and unlike anything a political candidate has ever said in American history.<sup id="cite_ref-NYMag_Never_Sounded_Like_This_392-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYMag_Never_Sounded_Like_This-392"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Lerer_10152024_389-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lerer_10152024-389"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>387<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Applebaum18_393-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Applebaum18-393"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>391<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Age_and_health_concerns_about_Donald_Trump" title="Age and health concerns about Donald Trump">Age and health concerns</a> also arose during the campaign, with several medical experts highlighting an increase in rambling, <a href="/wiki/Tangential_speech" title="Tangential speech">tangential speech</a> and <a href="/wiki/Disinhibition" title="Disinhibition">behavioral disinhibition</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-394" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-394"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>392<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump mentioned "rigged election" and "election interference" earlier and more frequently than in the 2016 and 2020 campaigns and refused to commit to accepting the 2024 election results.<sup id="cite_ref-395" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-395"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>393<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-NYTDoubts_396-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYTDoubts-396"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>394<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> On July 13, 2024, Trump was shot in the ear in <a href="/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Donald_Trump_in_Pennsylvania" title="Attempted assassination of Donald Trump in Pennsylvania">an assassination attempt</a> at a campaign rally in <a href="/wiki/Butler_Township,_Butler_County,_Pennsylvania" title="Butler Township, Butler County, Pennsylvania">Butler Township, Pennsylvania</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Browne-2024_397-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Browne-2024-397"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>395<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-398" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-398"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>396<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-399" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-399"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>397<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Two days later, the <a href="/wiki/2024_Republican_National_Convention" title="2024 Republican National Convention">2024 Republican National Convention</a> nominated him as their presidential candidate.<sup id="cite_ref-400" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-400"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>398<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In September he was targeted but unharmed in <a href="/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Donald_Trump_in_Florida" title="Attempted assassination of Donald Trump in Florida">another assassination attempt</a> in Florida.<sup id="cite_ref-401" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-401"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>399<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump won the election in November 2024 with 312 electoral votes to incumbent vice president Kamala Harris's 226.<sup id="cite_ref-402" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-402"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>400<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He also won the popular vote with 49.8% to Harris's 48.3%.<sup id="cite_ref-403" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-403"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>401<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His victory in 2024 was in large part due to the <a href="/wiki/2021%E2%80%932023_inflation_surge" title="2021–2023 inflation surge">2021–2023 inflation surge</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Global_Politics_404-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Global_Politics-404"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-405" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-405"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>403<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Several outlets described his reelection as an extraordinary comeback.<sup id="cite_ref-406" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-406"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>404<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-407" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-407"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>405<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Second_presidency_(2025–present)"><span id="Second_presidency_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Second presidency (2025–present)</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump" title="Second presidency of Donald Trump">Second presidency of Donald Trump</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">For a chronological guide, see <a href="/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Donald_Trump_presidencies#Second_presidency_(2025–present)" title="Timeline of the Donald Trump presidencies">Timeline of the Donald Trump presidencies § Second presidency (2025–present)</a>.</div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_(2025)_(alternate).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_%282025%29_%28alternate%29.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_%282025%29_%28alternate%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_%282025%29_%28alternate%29.jpg/500px-Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_%282025%29_%28alternate%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4200" data-file-height="2798"></a><figcaption>Taking the <a href="/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States" class="mw-redirect" title="Oath of office of the President of the United States">presidential oath of office</a>, administered by Chief Justice <a href="/wiki/John_Roberts" title="John Roberts">John Roberts</a>, on January 20, 2025</figcaption></figure>
<p>Trump began his second term upon <a href="/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Donald_Trump" title="Second inauguration of Donald Trump">his inauguration</a> on January 20, 2025.<sup id="cite_ref-408" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-408"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>406<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He became the <a href="/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_by_age" title="List of presidents of the United States by age">oldest to assume the presidency</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-409" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-409"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>407<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> the first president with a felony conviction, and the second person to serve two nonconsecutive terms as president.<sup id="cite_ref-410" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-410"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>408<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump chose several Project 2025 authors for his second administration;<sup id="cite_ref-411" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-411"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>409<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> after the first year of his second term about half of the project's agenda had been implemented.<sup id="cite_ref-412" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-412"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>410<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-413" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-413"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>411<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Emphasizing his conflicts with judicial authority, <i>The New York Times</i> said his attempts to expand presidential power are a defining characteristic of his second term.<sup id="cite_ref-414" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-414"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>412<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Early_actions">Early actions</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/First_100_days_of_the_second_Trump_presidency" title="First 100 days of the second Trump presidency">First 100 days of the second Trump presidency</a></div>
<p>More than a month before the 100-day mark of Trump's second term, he had <a href="/wiki/List_of_executive_orders_in_the_second_Trump_presidency" title="List of executive orders in the second Trump presidency">issued more executive orders</a> than any other U.S. president's first 100 days.<sup id="cite_ref-415" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-415"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>413<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> As of mid-January 2026, his orders and actions had been challenged in over 550 lawsuits nationwide. Of these, plaintiffs had prevailed in 195 cases, the government had prevailed in 109 cases, there were split rulings in 16 cases, 228 cases were pending, and 25 cases were closed.<sup id="cite_ref-416" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-416"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Both Republican and Democratic judicial appointees have found numerous constitutional and statutory flaws with Trump administration policies.<sup id="cite_ref-417" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-417"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>415<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Following legal setbacks, Trump increased his criticism of the judiciary and called for impeachment of federal judges who ruled against him.<sup id="cite_ref-418" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-418"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>416<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> By mid-July 2025, a <i><a href="/wiki/Washington_Post" class="mw-redirect" title="Washington Post">Washington Post</a></i> analysis found he defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him, actions which were described by legal experts as unprecedented for any presidential administration.<sup id="cite_ref-419" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-419"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>417<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>His administration asserted a constitutional right to ignore federal law in its justification of several actions such as in his refusal to enforce a <a href="/wiki/Protecting_Americans_from_Foreign_Adversary_Controlled_Applications_Act" title="Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act">federal ban on TikTok</a>, with legal experts describing it as claiming a "constitutional power to immunize private parties to commit otherwise illegal acts with impunity".<sup id="cite_ref-420" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-420"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>418<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-421" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-421"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>419<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-422" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-422"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>420<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Conflicts_of_interest_(2025–present)"><span id="Conflicts_of_interest_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Conflicts of interest (2025–present)</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump#Ethics" title="Second presidency of Donald Trump">Second presidency of Donald Trump § Ethics</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Cryptocurrency_in_the_second_Trump_presidency" title="Cryptocurrency in the second Trump presidency">Cryptocurrency in the second Trump presidency</a></div>
<p>Trump's second presidency has been described as having fewer guardrails against conflicts of interest than his first,<sup id="cite_ref-Yourish_2025_423-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Yourish_2025-423"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Schouten_2025_424-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Schouten_2025-424"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>422<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> breaking with decades of ethical norms,<sup id="cite_ref-425" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-425"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>423<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and raising substantial <a href="/wiki/Corruption" title="Corruption">corruption</a> concerns.<sup id="cite_ref-Savage_05122025_426-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Savage_05122025-426"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>424<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-427" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-427"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>425<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He maintained a publicly traded company in <a href="/wiki/Trump_Media_%26_Technology_Group" title="Trump Media & Technology Group">Trump Media & Technology Group</a>, and diversified it into financial services.<sup id="cite_ref-428" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-428"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>426<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He pursued new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities, and had several branding and licensing deals selling Trump-branded merchandise.<sup id="cite_ref-Yourish_2025_423-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Yourish_2025-423"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He profited from events held at his hotels and golf courses and did not place his assets in a blind trust, as previous presidents had done.<sup id="cite_ref-Schouten_1232025_429-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Schouten_1232025-429"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>427<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from <a href="/wiki/The_Trump_Organization#Other_ventures_and_investments" title="The Trump Organization">two cryptocurrency tokens</a> ("<a href="/wiki/Meme_coin" title="Meme coin">meme coins</a>"), <a href="/wiki/$Trump" title="$Trump">$Trump</a> and $Melania.<sup id="cite_ref-Weissert_1222025_430-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Weissert_1222025-430"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>428<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-431" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-431"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>429<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He also directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company <a href="/wiki/World_Liberty_Financial" title="World Liberty Financial">World Liberty Financial</a>, which engaged in an unprecedented mixing of private enterprise and government policy.<sup id="cite_ref-Lipton_04292025_432-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lipton_04292025-432"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>430<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Mass_terminations_of_federal_employees">Mass terminations of federal employees</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/2025_United_States_federal_mass_layoffs" title="2025 United States federal mass layoffs">2025 United States federal mass layoffs</a></div>
<p>Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days.<sup id="cite_ref-433" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-433"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>431<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-upend_434-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-upend-434"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>432<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of <a href="/wiki/Policy/Career_appointment" class="mw-redirect" title="Policy/Career appointment">reclassifying them into at-will positions</a> without job protections.<sup id="cite_ref-upend_434-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-upend-434"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>432<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-435" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-435"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>433<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-436" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-436"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>434<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He initiated mass job terminations of federal employees,<sup id="cite_ref-Collinson_437-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Collinson-437"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>435<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law,<sup id="cite_ref-438" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-438"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>436<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> with the intent of replacing them with workers more aligned with his agenda.<sup id="cite_ref-439" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-439"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>437<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> By late February 2025, the administration had fired more than 30,000 people.<sup id="cite_ref-440" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-440"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>438<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He ordered an end to <a href="/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion" title="Diversity, equity, and inclusion">diversity, equity, and inclusion</a> (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded <a href="/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson" title="Lyndon B. Johnson">Lyndon B. Johnson</a>'s 1965 <a href="/wiki/Executive_Order_11246" title="Executive Order 11246">Executive Order 11246</a>, which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination.<sup id="cite_ref-441" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-441"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>439<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-442" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-442"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>440<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump and <a href="/wiki/Elon_Musk" title="Elon Musk">Elon Musk</a>'s <a href="/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency" title="Department of Government Efficiency">Department of Government Efficiency</a> largely dismantled several federal agencies including <a href="/wiki/USAID" class="mw-redirect" title="USAID">USAID</a> and the <a href="/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education" title="United States Department of Education">Department of Education</a>, unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums.<sup id="cite_ref-443" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-443"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>441<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-444" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-444"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>442<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-445" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-445"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>443<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Targeting_political_opponents">Targeting political opponents</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Targeting_of_political_opponents_and_civil_society_under_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration">Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration</a></div>
<p>During his second presidency, the Trump administration took a series of actions using the government to target political opponents and <a href="/wiki/Civil_society" title="Civil society">civil society</a>. He threatened, signed executive actions, and ordered investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party.<sup id="cite_ref-446" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-446"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>444<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He ended the post-<a href="/wiki/Watergate" class="mw-redirect" title="Watergate">Watergate</a> norm of <a href="/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice" title="United States Department of Justice">Justice Department</a> independence, weaponizing it and agencies across the federal government to target his political enemies.<sup id="cite_ref-447" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-447"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>445<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-448" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-448"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>446<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as <a href="/wiki/Authoritarian" class="mw-redirect" title="Authoritarian">authoritarian</a>, contributing to <a href="/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States" title="Democratic backsliding in the United States">democratic backsliding</a>, and negatively impacting the <a href="/wiki/Rule_of_law" title="Rule of law">rule of law</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-449" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-449"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>447<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-450" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-450"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>448<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-451" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-451"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>449<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Pardons_and_commutations_(2025–present)"><span id="Pardons_and_commutations_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Pardons and commutations (2025–present)</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/List_of_people_granted_executive_clemency_in_the_second_Trump_presidency" title="List of people granted executive clemency in the second Trump presidency">List of people granted executive clemency in the second Trump presidency</a></div>
<p>Trump's pardons and grants of clemency favored political allies and loyalists,<sup id="cite_ref-452" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-452"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>450<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Thrush_May292025_453-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Thrush_May292025-453"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>451<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and disproportionately pardoned "the powerful, famous, well-connected and wealthy" accused of white-collar crime.<sup id="cite_ref-Thrush_May292025_453-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Thrush_May292025-453"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>451<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump <a href="/wiki/Pardon_of_January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack_defendants" title="Pardon of January 6 United States Capitol attack defendants">granted clemency to all January 6 rioters</a> convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14.<sup id="cite_ref-Reilly2025_454-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Reilly2025-454"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>452<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-NPR2025_455-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NPR2025-455"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>453<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Domestic_policy_(2025–present)"><span id="Domestic_policy_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Domestic policy (2025–present)</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Domestic_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Domestic policy of the second Trump administration">Domestic policy of the second Trump administration</a>, <a href="/wiki/Economic_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Economic policy of the second Trump administration">Economic policy of the second Trump administration</a>, <a href="/wiki/Education_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Education policy of the second Trump administration">Education policy of the second Trump administration</a>, <a href="/wiki/Science_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Science policy of the second Trump administration">Science policy of the second Trump administration</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Persecution_of_transgender_people_under_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration">Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration</a></div>
<p>Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law.<sup id="cite_ref-456" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-456"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>454<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-457" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-457"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>455<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative <a href="/wiki/Free_market" title="Free market">free market</a> orthodoxy,<sup id="cite_ref-458" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-458"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>456<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> engaging in <a href="/wiki/State_capitalism" title="State capitalism">state capitalism</a> by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations.<sup id="cite_ref-459" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-459"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>457<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Economist_08132025_460-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Economist_08132025-460"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>458<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-461" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-461"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>459<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Science">Science</h4></div>
<p>Trump <a href="/wiki/2025_United_States_federal_government_grant_pause" title="2025 United States federal government grant pause">canceled and paused federal grants</a> and made large cuts to scientific research,<sup id="cite_ref-462" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-462"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>460<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> several of which were found by judges and the <a href="/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office" class="mw-redirect" title="Government Accountability Office">Government Accountability Office</a> as being illegal and unconstitutional.<sup id="cite_ref-463" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-463"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>461<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-464" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-464"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>462<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump and his administration's <a href="/wiki/Make_America_Healthy_Again" title="Make America Healthy Again">Make America Healthy Again</a> agenda promoted <a href="/wiki/Anti-science" class="mw-redirect" title="Anti-science">anti-science</a> and <a href="/wiki/Anti-vaccine_activism" title="Anti-vaccine activism">anti-vaccine activism</a>, resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles.<sup id="cite_ref-465" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-465"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>463<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-466" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-466"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>464<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="National_emergencies/health_policy"><span id="National_emergencies.2Fhealth_policy"></span>National emergencies/health policy</h4></div>
<p>Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review.<sup id="cite_ref-467" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-467"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>465<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> For instance, he declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction and limiting renewable energy projects.<sup id="cite_ref-468" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-468"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>466<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-469" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-469"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>467<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump withdrew the U.S. from the <a href="/wiki/Paris_Agreement" title="Paris Agreement">Paris Agreement</a> on climate change during his first presidency, <a href="/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden" title="Presidency of Joe Biden">Biden</a> reentered the agreement in 2021, and Trump again withdrew from the agreement during his second presidency.<sup id="cite_ref-470" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-470"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>468<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Social">Social</h4></div>
<p>Trump attributed societal problems to <a href="/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion" title="Diversity, equity, and inclusion">diversity, equity, and inclusion</a> (DEI) initiatives and <a href="/wiki/Woke#United_States" title="Woke">wokeness</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Green250203_471-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Green250203-471"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>469<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Equating diversity with incompetence,<sup id="cite_ref-Green250203_471-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Green250203-471"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>469<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> he reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government.<sup id="cite_ref-472" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-472"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>470<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-473" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-473"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>471<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He threatened cultural institutions on DEI grounds<sup id="cite_ref-474" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-474"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>472<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and sixty universities on accusations of antisemitism,<sup id="cite_ref-WSJed_475-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WSJed-475"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>473<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and forced law firms to capitulate to his political agenda.<sup id="cite_ref-APfirms_476-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-APfirms-476"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>474<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered <a href="/wiki/2025_deployment_of_federal_forces_in_the_United_States" class="mw-redirect" title="2025 deployment of federal forces in the United States">military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities</a>, and threatened to expand his deployments further.<sup id="cite_ref-477" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-477"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>475<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-478" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-478"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>476<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Through <a href="/wiki/Persecution_of_transgender_people_under_the_second_Trump_administration#Executive_Orders" title="Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration">a series of executive orders</a> and other actions, Trump banned transgender people from the military;<sup id="cite_ref-479" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-479"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>477<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare; opposed <a href="/wiki/Inclusive_language" title="Inclusive language">inclusive language</a>;<sup id="cite_ref-480" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-480"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>478<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> censored research and education materials;<sup id="cite_ref-481" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-481"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>479<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls "gender ideology";<sup id="cite_ref-npr-ideology_482-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-npr-ideology-482"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>480<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> barred transgender athletes from sports; and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth.<sup id="cite_ref-483" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-483"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>481<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="One_Big_Beautiful_Bill_Act">One Big Beautiful Bill Act</h4></div>
<p>In July 2025, Trump signed the <a href="/wiki/One_Big_Beautiful_Bill_Act" title="One Big Beautiful Bill Act">One Big Beautiful Bill Act</a> into law. The bill made the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 <a href="/wiki/Tax_Cuts_and_Jobs_Act" title="Tax Cuts and Jobs Act">Tax Cuts and Jobs Act</a> permanent and added additional tax deductions for a total of around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets and costing people in the lowest income bracket $1,600 per year. It increased funding for national defense, deportations, the border wall, and Trump's proposed <a href="/wiki/Golden_Dome_(missile_defense_system)" title="Golden Dome (missile defense system)">Golden Dome</a> missile defense system. It removed tax credits for renewable energy sources such as wind and solar power and for buyers of electric vehicles. The bill cut funding for <a href="/wiki/Medicaid" title="Medicaid">Medicaid</a> and <a href="/wiki/Supplemental_Nutrition_Assistance_Program" title="Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program">SNAP</a> and added additional work requirements for eligibility and a $35 co-payment for some Medicaid services; the cuts and additional requirements will take effect after the 2026 general election. The bill was projected by the <a href="/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office" title="Congressional Budget Office">Congressional Budget Office</a> to increase the budget deficit by $3.4 trillion by 2034, cause 11.8 million people to lose Medicaid coverage, and eliminate SNAP benefits for three million people.<sup id="cite_ref-484" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-484"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>482<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-485" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-485"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>483<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Immigration_(2025–present)"><span id="Immigration_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Immigration (2025–present)</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Immigration policy of the second Trump administration">Immigration policy of the second Trump administration</a> and <a href="/wiki/Deportation_in_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Deportation in the second Trump administration">Deportation in the second Trump administration</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_crisis#Second_Trump_administration_(2025–present)" title="Mexico–United States border crisis">Mexico–United States border crisis § Second Trump administration (2025–present)</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall#Second_Trump_administration_(2025–present)" title="Mexico–United States border wall">Mexico–United States border wall § Second Trump administration (2025–present)</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Visa_and_deportation_controversies_in_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Visa and deportation controversies in the second Trump administration">Visa and deportation controversies in the second Trump administration</a></div>
<p>During Trump's first days in office, he instructed Border Patrol agents to summarily deport <a href="/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States" title="Illegal immigration to the United States">illegal immigrants</a> crossing the border and disabled the <a href="/wiki/CBP_One" title="CBP One">CBP One</a> app that was being used to schedule border crossings. Trump resumed the <a href="/wiki/Remain_in_Mexico" title="Remain in Mexico">Remain in Mexico</a> policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.<sup id="cite_ref-486" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-486"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>484<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Cbsjan28_487-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cbsjan28-487"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>485<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He also revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended <a href="/wiki/Parole_for_Cubans,_Haitians,_Nicaraguans,_and_Venezuelans" title="Parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans, and Venezuelans">humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Cbsjan28_487-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cbsjan28-487"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>485<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In March 2025, he used the <a href="/wiki/Alien_Enemies_Act" class="mw-redirect" title="Alien Enemies Act">Alien Enemies Act</a> of 1798 to <a href="/wiki/March_2025_American_deportations_of_Venezuelans" title="March 2025 American deportations of Venezuelans">deport migrants without trial</a> to be imprisoned at the <a href="/wiki/Terrorism_Confinement_Center" title="Terrorism Confinement Center">Terrorism Confinement Center</a> in El Salvador.<sup id="cite_ref-488" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-488"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>486<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.<sup id="cite_ref-489" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-489"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>487<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Several <a href="/wiki/Detention_and_deportation_of_American_citizens_in_the_second_Trump_administration" class="mw-redirect" title="Detention and deportation of American citizens in the second Trump administration">American citizens were detained and deported</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-490" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-490"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>488<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters which increased in intensity following high-profile killings of U.S. citizens by federal agents during <a href="/wiki/Operation_Metro_Surge" title="Operation Metro Surge">Operation Metro Surge</a> in January 2026.<sup id="cite_ref-491" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-491"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>489<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-492" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-492"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>490<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Foreign_policy_(2025–present)"><span id="Foreign_policy_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Foreign policy (2025–present)</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Foreign policy of the second Trump administration">Foreign policy of the second Trump administration</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Tariffs in the second Trump administration">Tariffs in the second Trump administration</a>, <a href="/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war" title="China–United States trade war">China–United States trade war</a>, <a href="/wiki/2025%E2%80%932026_United_States_trade_war_with_Canada_and_Mexico" title="2025–2026 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico">2025–2026 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico</a>, <a href="/wiki/American_expansionism_under_Donald_Trump" title="American expansionism under Donald Trump">American expansionism under Donald Trump</a>, and <a href="/wiki/2026_Iran_war" title="2026 Iran war">2026 Iran war</a></div>
<p>Trump's second-term foreign policy was described as <a href="/wiki/American_expansionism_under_Donald_Trump" title="American expansionism under Donald Trump">expansionist</a> and imperialist.<sup id="cite_ref-493" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-493"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>491<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-494" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-494"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>492<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His administration favored <a href="/wiki/Hard_power" title="Hard power">hard power</a> to achieve its <a href="/wiki/America_First" title="America First">America First</a> foreign policy goals,<sup id="cite_ref-495" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-495"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>493<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and dismantled or withdrew support from domestic and international organizations dedicated to advancing American <a href="/wiki/Soft_power" title="Soft power">soft power</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-496" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-496"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>494<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-497" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-497"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>495<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump's relations with U.S. allies have been transactional and ranged from indifference to hostility, while he has sought friendlier relations with certain U.S. adversaries.<sup id="cite_ref-498" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-498"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>496<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><span class="mw-tmh-player video"><video id="mwe_player_1_placeholder" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/President_Trump_and_Ukrainian_President_Zelenskyy_in_Oval_Office%2C_Feb._28%2C_2025.webm/250px-seek%3D2594-President_Trump_and_Ukrainian_President_Zelenskyy_in_Oval_Office%2C_Feb._28%2C_2025.webm.jpg" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="2962" data-mwtitle="President_Trump_and_Ukrainian_President_Zelenskyy_in_Oval_Office,_Feb._28,_2025.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:President_Trump_and_Ukrainian_President_Zelenskyy_in_Oval_Office,_Feb._28,_2025.webm" playsinline="" disabled="disabled" tabindex="-1" style="aspect-ratio: 250 / 141;"></video><a class="mw-tmh-play" href="/wiki/File:President_Trump_and_Ukrainian_President_Zelenskyy_in_Oval_Office,_Feb._28,_2025.webm" title="Play video" role="button"><span class="mw-tmh-play-icon notheme"></span></a></span></span><a href="/wiki/File:President_Trump_and_Ukrainian_President_Zelenskyy_in_Oval_Office,_Feb._28,_2025.webm" class="mw-file-magnify"></a><figcaption>Meeting with Zelenskyy, February 2025</figcaption></figure>
<p>In February 2025, Trump and Vice President Vance <a href="/wiki/2025_Trump%E2%80%93Zelenskyy_Oval_Office_meeting" title="2025 Trump–Zelenskyy Oval Office meeting">berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy</a> in a highly contentious televised meeting. Media outlets described it as an unprecedented public confrontation between an American president and a foreign <a href="/wiki/Head_of_state" title="Head of state">head of state</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Liptak_20250228_499-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Liptak_20250228-499"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>497<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-500" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-500"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>498<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump has taken a pro-Israel stance and continued <a href="/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war" title="United States support for Israel in the Gaza war">support for Israel in the Gaza war</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-501" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-501"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>499<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> From March to May 2025, Trump <a href="/wiki/March%E2%80%93May_2025_United_States_attacks_in_Yemen" title="March–May 2025 United States attacks in Yemen">launched an extensive aerial campaign</a> against <a href="/wiki/Houthis" title="Houthis">Houthi</a> targets in Yemen—his first major military operation in the Middle East during his second term.<sup id="cite_ref-502" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-502"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>500<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In June 2025, Trump joined Israel in its <a href="/wiki/Twelve-Day_War" title="Twelve-Day War">Twelve-Day War</a> with Iran, ordering the <a href="/wiki/2025_United_States_strikes_on_Iranian_nuclear_sites" title="2025 United States strikes on Iranian nuclear sites">bombing of Iranian nuclear sites</a> by U.S. planes.<sup id="cite_ref-503" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-503"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>501<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His <a href="/wiki/Gaza_peace_plan" title="Gaza peace plan">plan for a Gaza ceasefire deal</a> between Israel and <a href="/wiki/Hamas" title="Hamas">Hamas</a> was signed in October 2025, leading to the creation of the <a href="/wiki/Board_of_Peace" title="Board of Peace">Board of Peace</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-504" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-504"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>502<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In February 2026, Trump authorized <a href="/wiki/2026_Iran_war" title="2026 Iran war">joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran</a><sup id="cite_ref-505" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-505"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>503<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and sent aircraft carriers and other combat ships to the Middle East. Israeli aerial bombs killed Iranian supreme leader <a href="/wiki/Ali_Khamenei" title="Ali Khamenei">Ali Khamenei</a> and other Iranian leaders; Iran retaliated with rocket attacks on neighboring countries.<sup id="cite_ref-506" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-506"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>504<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump's economic policies have been described as <a href="/wiki/Protectionism_in_the_United_States" title="Protectionism in the United States">protectionist</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-507" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-507"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>505<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> with him imposing tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.<sup id="cite_ref-508" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-508"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>506<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He started a global trade war, imposing tariffs at the highest level since the 1930 <a href="/wiki/Smoot%E2%80%93Hawley_Tariff_Act" title="Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act">Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act</a> at the onset of the <a href="/wiki/Great_Depression" title="Great Depression">Great Depression</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-509" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-509"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>507<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-510" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-510"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>508<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In February 2026, the Supreme Court ruled the tariffs he imposed under the <a href="/wiki/International_Emergency_Economic_Powers_Act" title="International Emergency Economic Powers Act">International Emergency Economic Powers Act</a> to be illegal.<sup id="cite_ref-511" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-511"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>509<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump frequently threatened and enacted tariffs against treaty allies for opposing his political objectives.<sup id="cite_ref-512" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-512"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>510<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-513" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-513"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>511<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In Latin America, Trump pursued legally controversial military strikes against <a href="/wiki/United_States_strikes_on_alleged_drug_traffickers_during_Operation_Southern_Spear" title="United States strikes on alleged drug traffickers during Operation Southern Spear">alleged drug boats</a> in international waters,<sup id="cite_ref-514" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-514"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>512<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and ordered <a href="/wiki/United_States_military_buildup_in_the_Caribbean_during_Operation_Southern_Spear" title="United States military buildup in the Caribbean during Operation Southern Spear">a large military buildup</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-515" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-515"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>513<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In November 2025, Trump pardoned former Honduran president <a href="/wiki/Juan_Orlando_Hern%C3%A1ndez" title="Juan Orlando Hernández">Juan Orlando Hernández</a>, who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking. In January 2026, the U.S. captured Venezuelan president <a href="/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro" title="Nicolás Maduro">Nicolás Maduro</a> in <a href="/wiki/2026_United_States_strikes_in_Venezuela#Capture_of_Maduro" class="mw-redirect" title="2026 United States strikes in Venezuela">a military raid in Venezuela</a> and took him to New York, where he was charged with drug trafficking.<sup id="cite_ref-516" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-516"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>514<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2025, Trump <a href="/wiki/Greenland_crisis" title="Greenland crisis">threatened the annexation of Greenland</a>, including by military force or the imposition of tariffs on European allies. In January 2026, he appeared to withdraw both threats, stating that he had reached "the framework of a future deal" with NATO.<sup id="cite_ref-517" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-517"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>515<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Personnel_(2025–present)"><span id="Personnel_.282025.E2.80.93present.29"></span>Personnel (2025–present)</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Political_appointments_of_the_second_Trump_administration" title="Political appointments of the second Trump administration">Political appointments of the second Trump administration</a> and <a href="/wiki/Second_cabinet_of_Donald_Trump" title="Second cabinet of Donald Trump">Second cabinet of Donald Trump</a></div>
<p>In his second term, Trump selected <a href="/wiki/Second_cabinet_of_Donald_Trump" title="Second cabinet of Donald Trump">cabinet</a> members with personal loyalty to him,<sup id="cite_ref-518" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-518"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>516<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-focus_519-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-focus-519"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>517<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> with the "focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise".<sup id="cite_ref-focus_519-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-focus-519"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>517<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In February 2025, the White House stated that <a href="/wiki/Elon_Musk" title="Elon Musk">Elon Musk</a> was a <a href="/wiki/Special_government_employee" title="Special government employee">special government employee</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-musk_520-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-musk-520"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>518<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump gave Musk's <a href="/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency" title="Department of Government Efficiency">Department of Government Efficiency</a> (DOGE) access to many federal government agencies.<sup id="cite_ref-musk_520-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-musk-520"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>518<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Musk's teams operated in eighteen departments and agencies in the administration's first month,<sup id="cite_ref-521" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-521"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>519<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> including in the Treasury Department's $5 trillion payment system,<sup id="cite_ref-522" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-522"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>520<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> the <a href="/wiki/Small_Business_Administration" title="Small Business Administration">Small Business Administration</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Office_of_Personnel_Management" class="mw-redirect" title="Office of Personnel Management">Office of Personnel Management</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/General_Services_Administration" title="General Services Administration">General Services Administration</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-523" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-523"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>521<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Rhetoric,_behavior,_and_political_practice"><span id="Rhetoric.2C_behavior.2C_and_political_practice"></span>Rhetoric, behavior, and political practice</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Political_positions_of_Donald_Trump" title="Political positions of Donald Trump">Political positions of Donald Trump</a> and <a href="/wiki/Rhetoric_of_Donald_Trump" title="Rhetoric of Donald Trump">Rhetoric of Donald Trump</a></div>
<p>Starting with his advocacy of <a href="/wiki/Birther" class="mw-redirect" title="Birther">birther</a> conspiracy theories in the 2010s, Trump helped bring once-fringe far-right ideas and organizations into the mainstream.<sup id="cite_ref-524" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-524"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>522<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
The <a href="/wiki/Alt-right" title="Alt-right">alt-right</a> movement coalesced around and supported his candidacy due in part to <a href="/wiki/Opposition_to_multiculturalism" class="mw-redirect" title="Opposition to multiculturalism">opposition to multiculturalism</a> and <a href="/wiki/Opposition_to_immigration" title="Opposition to immigration">immigration</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-525" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-525"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>523<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-526" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-526"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>524<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-527" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-527"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>525<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
During his 2016 campaign, Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a <a href="/wiki/Political_movement" title="Political movement">political movement</a> known as <a href="/wiki/Trumpism" title="Trumpism">Trumpism</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-528" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-528"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>526<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which has been <a href="/wiki/Trumpism#Cult_of_personality" title="Trumpism">compared to a cult of personality</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-529" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-529"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>527<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
