His political positions have been described as <a href="/wiki/Populist" class="mw-redirect" title="Populist">populist</a><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERoss2024298_530-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2024298-530"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>528<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEUrbinati2019_531-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUrbinati2019-531"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>529<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> or <a href="/wiki/Right-wing_populist" class="mw-redirect" title="Right-wing populist">right-wing populist</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTECampaniConcepciónSolerSavín2022_532-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampaniConcepciónSolerSavín2022-532"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>530<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-533" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-533"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>531<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and favoring an expansion of presidential power under a maximalist interpretation of the <a href="/wiki/Unitary_executive_theory" title="Unitary executive theory">unitary executive theory</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-534" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-534"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>532<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-535" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-535"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>533<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Many of his actions and rhetoric have been described as <a href="/wiki/Authoritarian" class="mw-redirect" title="Authoritarian">authoritarian</a> and contributing to <a href="/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States" title="Democratic backsliding in the United States">democratic backsliding</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEParkerTowler2019505;_513_536-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParkerTowler2019505;_513-536"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>534<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEKaufmanHaggard2019_537-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaufmanHaggard2019-537"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>535<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERoss2024300"Donald_Trump_is_broadly_seen_as_posing_an_existential_threat_to_American_democracy."_538-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2024300"Donald_Trump_is_broadly_seen_as_posing_an_existential_threat_to_American_democracy."-538"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>536<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> as well as establishing an <a href="/wiki/In-group_and_out-group" title="In-group and out-group">"us versus them" narrative</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERoss2024299_539-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2024299-539"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>537<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump's rhetoric has been described as using <a href="/wiki/Fearmongering" title="Fearmongering">fearmongering</a> and <a href="/wiki/Demagogy" class="mw-redirect" title="Demagogy">demagogy</a> which <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_2024_presidential_campaign#Fearmongering" title="Donald Trump 2024 presidential campaign">intensified during his 2024 presidential campaign</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-540" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-540"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>538<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He appeals to <a href="/wiki/Trumpism#Christian_Trumpism" title="Trumpism">evangelical Christians</a> and <a href="/wiki/Christian_nationalism_in_the_United_States" title="Christian nationalism in the United States">Christian nationalists</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEPerryWhiteheadGrubbs2021229Christian_nationalist_ideology_was_among_the_strongest_predictors_of_Americans_voting_for_Trump_in_2016_and_remained_a_strong_predictor_of_intent_to_vote_for_him_prior_to_the_2020_election_541-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerryWhiteheadGrubbs2021229Christian_nationalist_ideology_was_among_the_strongest_predictors_of_Americans_voting_for_Trump_in_2016_and_remained_a_strong_predictor_of_intent_to_vote_for_him_prior_to_the_2020_election-541"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>539<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-542" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-542"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>540<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and regularly vilifies his political opponents as "evil".<sup id="cite_ref-543" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-543"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>541<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Race_and_gender">Race and gender</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Racial_views_of_Donald_Trump" title="Racial views of Donald Trump">Racial views of Donald Trump</a> and <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_sexual_misconduct_allegations" title="Donald Trump sexual misconduct allegations">Donald Trump sexual misconduct allegations</a></div>
<p>Many of Trump's comments and actions have been characterized as racist.<sup id="cite_ref-544" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-544"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>542<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Several studies and surveys found that racist attitudes fueled Trump's political ascent and were more important than economic factors in determining the allegiance of Trump voters.<sup id="cite_ref-545" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-545"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>543<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He explicitly and routinely disparages racial, religious, and ethnic minorities,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEStephens-Dougan2021302_546-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephens-Dougan2021302-546"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>544<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and scholars consistently find that racist and <a href="/wiki/Islamophobic" class="mw-redirect" title="Islamophobic">Islamophobic</a> attitudes regarding blacks, immigrants, and Muslims are strong indicators of support for Trump.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTELajevardiOskooii2018_547-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTELajevardiOskooii2018-547"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>545<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBerman202176_548-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerman202176-548"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>546<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>  
</p><p>Trump has been accused of racism<sup id="cite_ref-549" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-549"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>547<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> for running full-page newspaper advertisements and insisting that a group of five black and Latino teenagers were guilty of raping a white woman in the 1989 <a href="/wiki/Central_Park_jogger_case" title="Central Park jogger case">Central Park jogger case</a>, even after they were cleared of suspicion in 2002.<sup id="cite_ref-550" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-550"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>548<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump's comments on the 2017 <a href="/wiki/Unite_the_Right_rally" title="Unite the Right rally">Unite the Right rally</a>, condemning "this egregious display of hatred, bigotry and violence on many sides" and stating that there were "very fine people on both sides", were criticized as implying a <a href="/wiki/Moral_equivalence" title="Moral equivalence">moral equivalence</a> between the <a href="/wiki/White_supremacist" class="mw-redirect" title="White supremacist">white supremacist</a> demonstrators and the counter-protesters.<sup id="cite_ref-551" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-551"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>549<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In a January 2018 discussion of immigration legislation, he reportedly referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as "shithole countries",<sup id="cite_ref-552" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-552"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>550<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> remarks condemned as racist.<sup id="cite_ref-553" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-553"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>551<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In July 2019, a House of Representatives <a href="/wiki/Racial_views_of_Donald_Trump#Democratic_congresswomen_should_"go_back"_to_their_countries" title="Racial views of Donald Trump">resolution condemned Trump for racist remarks</a> about four minority Democratic congresswomen.<sup id="cite_ref-554" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-554"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>552<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His 2024 campaign made extensive use of <a href="/wiki/Dehumanization" title="Dehumanization">dehumanizing language</a> and <a href="/wiki/Racial_stereotypes" class="mw-redirect" title="Racial stereotypes">racial stereotypes</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-555" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-555"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>553<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_(49964153176).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_%2849964153176%29.jpg/250px-President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_%2849964153176%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_%2849964153176%29.jpg/500px-President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_%2849964153176%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2800" data-file-height="1867"></a><figcaption>With a group of officials and advisors walking from the White House to St. John's Church, following the <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_photo_op_at_St._John%27s_Church#Clearing_Lafayette_Square_and_St._John's" title="Donald Trump photo op at St. John's Church">forced removal of protesters at Lafayette Square</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>In June 2020, during the <a href="/wiki/George_Floyd_protests" title="George Floyd protests">George Floyd protests</a>, federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from <a href="/wiki/Lafayette_Square,_Washington,_D.C." title="Lafayette Square, Washington, D.C.">Lafayette Square</a>, outside the <a href="/wiki/White_House" title="White House">White House</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-wb_556-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wb-556"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>554<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-bumpline_557-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bumpline-557"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>555<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump then <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_photo_op_at_St._John%27s_Church" title="Donald Trump photo op at St. John's Church">posed with a Bible for a photo op</a> at the nearby <a href="/wiki/St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church,_Lafayette_Square" class="mw-redirect" title="St. John's Episcopal Church, Lafayette Square">St. John's Episcopal Church</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-wb_556-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wb-556"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>554<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-558" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-558"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>556<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-559" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-559"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>557<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> with religious leaders condemning both the treatment of protesters and the photo opportunity itself.<sup id="cite_ref-560" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-560"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>558<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Trump has a history of belittling women when speaking to the media and on social media.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTERotheCollins2019_561-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTERotheCollins2019-561"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>559<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Shear_562-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Shear-562"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>560<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He made lewd comments, disparaged women's physical appearances, and referred to them using derogatory epithets.<sup id="cite_ref-Shear_562-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Shear-562"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>560<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In October 2016, a 2005 "<a href="/wiki/Hot_mic" title="Hot mic">hot mic</a>" recording surfaced in which <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_Access_Hollywood_tape" title="Donald Trump Access Hollywood tape">he bragged</a> about kissing and groping women without their consent, saying that, "when you're a star, they let you do it. You can do anything. ... Grab 'em by the <a href="/wiki/Pussy#Female_genitalia" title="Pussy">pussy</a>."<sup id="cite_ref-563" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-563"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>561<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He characterized the comments as "locker-room talk".<sup id="cite_ref-564" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-564"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>562<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-565" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-565"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>563<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The incident's widespread media exposure led to his first public apology, videotaped during his 2016 presidential campaign.<sup id="cite_ref-566" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-566"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>564<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> As of October 2024, since the 1970s, at least 28 women<sup id="cite_ref-NewRepublic10.31.24_567-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NewRepublic10.31.24-567"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>565<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> have accused Trump of various acts of sexual misconduct, including rape, sex with minors, sexual assault, physical abuse, kissing and groping without consent, looking under women's skirts, and walking in on naked pageant contestants.<sup id="cite_ref-no26_568-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-no26-568"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>566<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Link_to_violence_and_hate_crimes">Link to violence and hate crimes</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Rhetoric_of_Donald_Trump#Violence" title="Rhetoric of Donald Trump">Rhetoric of Donald Trump § Violence</a></div>
<p>Trump has been identified as a key figure in increasing political violence in the U.S., both for and against him.<sup id="cite_ref-569" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-569"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>567<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTENacosShapiroBloch-Elkon2020_570-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENacosShapiroBloch-Elkon2020-570"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>568<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEPiazzaVan_Doren2022_571-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiazzaVan_Doren2022-571"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>569<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He is described as embracing extremism, <a href="/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump" title="List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump">conspiracy theories</a> such as <a href="/wiki/QAnon" title="QAnon">QAnon</a>, and far-right <a href="/wiki/American_militia_movement" title="American militia movement">militia movements</a> to a greater extent than any other modern American president.<sup id="cite_ref-572" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-572"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>570<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-573" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-573"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>571<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Research suggests that Trump's rhetoric is associated with an increased incidence of hate crimes,<sup id="cite_ref-574" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-574"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>572<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-575" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-575"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>573<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and that he has an emboldening effect on expressing prejudicial attitudes due to his normalization of explicit racial rhetoric.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEStephens-Dougan2021306_576-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephens-Dougan2021306-576"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>574<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Researchers have also argued that Trump's "negative characterisations of, and false claims made about, those who became the targets" of the mob at the January 6 riot was a case of <a href="/wiki/Stochastic_terrorism" title="Stochastic terrorism">stochastic terrorism</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-577" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-577"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>575<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-578" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-578"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>576<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Numerous defendants investigated or prosecuted for violent acts and hate crimes cited his rhetoric in arguing that they were not culpable or should receive leniency.<sup id="cite_ref-579" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-579"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>577<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-580" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-580"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>578<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A nationwide review by <a href="/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States)" title="ABC News (United States)">ABC News</a> in May 2020 identified at least 54 criminal cases, from August 2015 to April 2020, in which he was invoked in direct connection with violence or threats of violence mostly by white men and primarily against minorities.<sup id="cite_ref-581" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-581"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>579<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump's refusal to condemn the white supremacist <a href="/wiki/Proud_Boys" title="Proud Boys">Proud Boys</a> during a 2020 presidential debate<sup id="cite_ref-582" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-582"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>580<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and his comment, "Proud Boys, stand back and stand by", were said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group.<sup id="cite_ref-583" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-583"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>581<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Counterterrorism researchers described his normalization and <a href="/wiki/Historical_revisionism" title="Historical revisionism">revisionist history</a> of the January 6 Capitol attack, and <a href="/wiki/Pardon_of_January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack_defendants" title="Pardon of January 6 United States Capitol attack defendants">grant of clemency to all January 6 rioters</a>, as encouraging future political violence.<sup id="cite_ref-584" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-584"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>582<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-585" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-585"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>583<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="False_or_misleading_statements_and_conspiracy_theories">False or misleading statements and conspiracy theories</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/False_or_misleading_statements_by_Donald_Trump" title="False or misleading statements by Donald Trump">False or misleading statements by Donald Trump</a> and <a href="/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump" title="List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump">List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Chart depicting false or misleading claims made by Trump" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png/500px-2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png" decoding="async" width="500" height="315" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.5" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png/960px-2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png 2x" data-file-width="2775" data-file-height="1750"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Fact-checkers" class="mw-redirect" title="Fact-checkers">Fact-checkers</a> from <i>The Washington Post</i>,<sup id="cite_ref-database_586-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-database-586"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>584<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> the <i>Toronto Star</i>,<sup id="cite_ref-587" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-587"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>585<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and CNN<sup id="cite_ref-588" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-588"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>586<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> compiled data on "false or misleading claims" (orange background) and "false claims" (violet foreground).</figcaption></figure>
<p>Trump frequently makes false statements in public remarks,<sup id="cite_ref-finnegan_589-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-finnegan-589"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>587<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-whoppers_126-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-whoppers-126"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> to an extent that was unprecedented in American politics.<sup id="cite_ref-finnegan_589-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-finnegan-589"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>587<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-glasser_590-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-glasser-590"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>588<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Konnikova_591-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Konnikova-591"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>589<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His false and misleading statements were documented by <a href="/wiki/Fact-checker" class="mw-redirect" title="Fact-checker">fact-checkers</a>, including at <i>The Washington Post</i>, which tallied 30,573 false or misleading statements made by him during his first presidency,<sup id="cite_ref-database_586-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-database-586"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>584<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> increasing in frequency over time.<sup id="cite_ref-TermUntruth_592-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-TermUntruth-592"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>590<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His falsehoods are a distinctive part of his political identity<sup id="cite_ref-glasser_590-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-glasser-590"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>588<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and have been described as <a href="/wiki/Firehosing" class="mw-redirect" title="Firehosing">firehosing</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEKakutani201894–104_593-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKakutani201894–104-593"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>591<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>During the first 100 days of his second term, Trump relied on false, misleading, and exaggerated claims to justify his executive actions and policies.<sup id="cite_ref-594" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-594"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>592<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Throughout the first year of the term, he continued to make untrue statements as frequently as before but with less variety while increasing the frequency of repeating a "core set" of falsehoods. <sup id="cite_ref-595" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-595"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>593<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some of Trump's falsehoods were inconsequential,<sup id="cite_ref-596" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-596"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>594<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-597" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-597"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>595<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> while others had more far-reaching effects, such as his unproven promotion of antimalarial drugs as a treatment for COVID-19,<sup id="cite_ref-598" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-598"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>596<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-599" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-599"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>597<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> contributing to a U.S. shortage of these drugs and <a href="/wiki/Panic_buying" title="Panic buying">panic buying</a> in Africa and South Asia.<sup id="cite_ref-600" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-600"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>598<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-601" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-601"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>599<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His attacks on mail-in ballots and other election practices weakened public faith in the integrity of the 2020 presidential election,<sup id="cite_ref-602" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-602"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>600<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-603" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-603"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>601<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> while his disinformation about the COVID-19 pandemic delayed and weakened the national response to it.<sup id="cite_ref-NYT_4_11_20_604-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT_4_11_20-604"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>602<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-USAT-Disinfo_605-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-USAT-Disinfo-605"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>603<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-606" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-606"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>604<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He habitually does not apologize for his falsehoods.<sup id="cite_ref-607" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-607"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>605<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Until 2018, the media rarely referred to his falsehoods as lies, including when he repeated demonstrably false statements.<sup id="cite_ref-608" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-608"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>606<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-DBauder_609-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-DBauder-609"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>607<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-610" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-610"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>608<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Since before his first presidency, Trump has <a href="/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump" title="List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump">promoted numerous conspiracy theories</a>. In 2011, Trump became the leading proponent of the racist <a href="/wiki/Barack_Obama_citizenship_conspiracy_theories" title="Barack Obama citizenship conspiracy theories">"birther" conspiracy theory</a> that Barack Obama, the first black U.S. president, was not born in the United States,<sup id="cite_ref-611" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-611"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>609<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and he claimed credit for pressuring the government to publish Obama's birth certificate, which he considered fraudulent.<sup id="cite_ref-612" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-612"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>610<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In September 2016, he publicly acknowledged <a href="/wiki/Barack_Obama" title="Barack Obama">Barack Obama</a>'s birthplace and falsely claimed that the rumors had been started by <a href="/wiki/Hillary_Clinton" title="Hillary Clinton">Hillary Clinton</a> during her <a href="/wiki/Hillary_Clinton_presidential_campaign,_2008" class="mw-redirect" title="Hillary Clinton presidential campaign, 2008">2008 presidential campaign</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-613" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-613"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>611<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Trump often prefaced his conspiracies by claiming he heard them from someone else,<sup id="cite_ref-Haberman2016_614-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Haberman2016-614"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>612<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and promoted conspiracies such as <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_denial" title="Climate change denial">climate change denial</a>, <a href="/wiki/Conspiracy_theories_related_to_the_2019_Trump%E2%80%93Ukraine_scandal" title="Conspiracy theories related to the 2019 Trump–Ukraine scandal">alleged Ukrainian interference in U.S. elections</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Vaccines_and_autism" title="Vaccines and autism">vaccines and autism</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Haberman2016_614-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Haberman2016-614"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>612<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-615" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-615"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>613<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-616" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-616"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>614<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> After the 2020 presidential election, he promoted conspiracy theories for his defeat that were characterized as "<a href="/wiki/Big_lie_(2020_United_States_presidential_election)" class="mw-redirect" title="Big lie (2020 United States presidential election)">the big lie</a>".<sup id="cite_ref-617" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-617"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>615<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-618" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-618"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>616<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Relationship_with_news_media">Relationship with news media</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump%27s_conflict_with_the_news_media" title="Donald Trump's conflict with the news media">Donald Trump's conflict with the news media</a></div>
<p>In the 2016 campaign, Trump benefited from a record amount of free media coverage,<sup id="cite_ref-Cillizza-160614_619-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cillizza-160614-619"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>617<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> estimated at $2 billion.<sup id="cite_ref-620" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-620"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>618<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> As a candidate and as president, he frequently accused the press of bias, calling it the "fake news media" and "the <a href="/wiki/Enemy_of_the_people" title="Enemy of the people">enemy of the people</a>".<sup id="cite_ref-621" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-621"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>619<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The first Trump presidency reduced formal press briefings from about one hundred in 2017 to two by 2019 and revoked the press passes of two White House reporters, which were restored by the courts.<sup id="cite_ref-622" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-622"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>620<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> By 2024, Trump repeatedly voiced support for <a href="/wiki/Criminalization_of_dissent" class="mw-redirect" title="Criminalization of dissent">outlawing political dissent</a> and criticism,<sup id="cite_ref-623" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-623"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>621<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.<sup id="cite_ref-624" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-624"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>622<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>In his second term, Trump's actions against the media were unprecedented in modern American history,<sup id="cite_ref-625" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-625"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>623<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and historians described them as mirroring actions by authoritarian leaders to censor political opponents and negatively impacting the <a href="/wiki/Freedom_of_speech" title="Freedom of speech">freedom of speech</a> and <a href="/wiki/Free_press" class="mw-redirect" title="Free press">free press</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-626" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-626"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>624<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-627" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-627"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>625<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The campaign to police speech drew comparisons to <a href="/wiki/Cancel_culture" title="Cancel culture">cancel culture</a>, government <a href="/wiki/Censorship" title="Censorship">censorship</a>, and <a href="/wiki/McCarthyism" title="McCarthyism">McCarthyism</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-628" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-628"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>626<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-629" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-629"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>627<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-630" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-630"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>628<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission" title="Federal Communications Commission">Federal Communications Commission</a> launched investigations into media outlets accused of bias against him.<sup id="cite_ref-631" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-631"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>629<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> As a result of Trump's threats, media executives instructed journalists and their staff to <a href="/wiki/Self-censor" class="mw-redirect" title="Self-censor">self-censor</a> and reduce criticism of Trump.<sup id="cite_ref-632" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-632"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>630<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Many have characterized Trump as causing a significant decline in <a href="/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press" title="Freedom of the press">freedom of the press</a>, including journalist advocacy groups<sup id="cite_ref-FerragamoCFR2025_633-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FerragamoCFR2025-633"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>631<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-RSFUnitedStates2025_634-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-RSFUnitedStates2025-634"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>632<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-CPJPressFreedomUS_635-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CPJPressFreedomUS-635"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>633<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-636" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-636"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>634<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and academic sources.<sup id="cite_ref-637" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-637"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>635<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-638" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-638"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>636<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-639" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-639"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>637<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Social_media">Social media</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Social_media_use_by_Donald_Trump" title="Social media use by Donald Trump">Social media use by Donald Trump</a> and <a href="/wiki/Use_of_X_by_Donald_Trump" title="Use of X by Donald Trump">Use of X by Donald Trump</a></div>
<p>During Trump's first term, he often used Twitter to communicate directly with the public and sideline the press.<sup id="cite_ref-gone_640-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-gone-640"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>638<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> After years of criticism for allowing Trump to post misinformation and falsehoods, Twitter began to tag some of his tweets with fact-checks in May 2020.<sup id="cite_ref-641" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-641"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>639<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Twitter banned him after the January 6 attack.<sup id="cite_ref-642" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-642"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>640<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He was banned from <a href="/wiki/Facebook" title="Facebook">Facebook</a>, <a href="/wiki/Instagram" title="Instagram">Instagram</a>, Twitter, and other platforms after the January 6 attack.<sup id="cite_ref-643" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-643"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>641<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The loss of his social media presence diminished his ability to shape events<sup id="cite_ref-644" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-644"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>642<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-645" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-645"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>643<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and correlated with a dramatic decrease in the volume of misinformation on Twitter.<sup id="cite_ref-646" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-646"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>644<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In February 2022, he launched social media platform <a href="/wiki/Truth_Social" title="Truth Social">Truth Social</a> where he attracted a fraction of his Twitter following.<sup id="cite_ref-647" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-647"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>645<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Twitter reinstated his account in November 2022.<sup id="cite_ref-648" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-648"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>646<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-649" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-649"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>647<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The two-year ban at <a href="/wiki/Meta_Platforms" title="Meta Platforms">Meta Platforms</a> lapsed in January 2023, allowing him to return to Facebook and Instagram.<sup id="cite_ref-650" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-650"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>648<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Assessments">Assessments</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Public_image">Public image</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Public_image_of_Donald_Trump" title="Public image of Donald Trump">Public image of Donald Trump</a> and <a href="/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_the_first_Trump_presidency" title="Opinion polling on the first Trump presidency">Opinion polling on the first Trump presidency</a></div>
<p>In Trump's first term, from 2017 to 2021, international approval ratings of U.S. leadership dropped from about 22 percent in a <a href="/wiki/Gallup,_Inc." title="Gallup, Inc.">Gallup</a> poll<sup id="cite_ref-651" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-651"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>649<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> of 134 countries to 16 percent in a <a href="/wiki/Pew_Research" class="mw-redirect" title="Pew Research">Pew Research</a> poll<sup id="cite_ref-652" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-652"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>650<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> of 13 countries. In 2017, estimation of U.S. leadership declined most among allies.<sup id="cite_ref-653" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-653"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>651<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Domestically, Trump had chiefly partisan support: 88 percent among Republicans and 7 percent among Democrats.<sup id="cite_ref-Jones_654-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jones-654"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>652<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In a 2021 Gallup poll, he was the only president never to reach a 50 percent approval rating, and he was the first not to be named most admired in his first year in office.<sup id="cite_ref-655" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-655"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>653<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Scholarly_rankings">Scholarly rankings</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States" title="Historical rankings of presidents of the United States">Historical rankings of presidents of the United States</a></div>
<p>After Trump's first term, <a href="/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#2021_C-SPAN" title="Historical rankings of presidents of the United States">historians ranked Trump</a> as the fourth-worst president in <a href="/wiki/C-SPAN" title="C-SPAN">C-SPAN</a>'s 2021 survey of presidential historians.<sup id="cite_ref-656" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-656"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>654<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He rated lowest in the leadership characteristics categories for moral authority and administrative skills.<sup id="cite_ref-657" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-657"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>655<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-658" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-658"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>656<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Siena_College_Research_Institute" class="mw-redirect" title="Siena College Research Institute">Siena College Research Institute</a>'s 2022 survey <a href="/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#2022_Siena_College" title="Historical rankings of presidents of the United States">ranked him</a> third-worst. He was ranked near the bottom in all categories except for luck, willingness to take risks, and party leadership, and ranked last in several categories.<sup id="cite_ref-scri_22_659-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-scri_22-659"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>657<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 2018 and 2024, the <a href="/wiki/American_Political_Science_Association" title="American Political Science Association">American Political Science Association</a> ranked him the worst president.<sup id="cite_ref-660" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-660"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>658<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-661" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-661"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>659<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Personal_life">Personal life</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Family">Family</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Trump_family" title="Trump family">Trump family</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Ivana Trump and King Fahd shake hands, with Ronald Reagan standing next to them smiling" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg/250px-Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="180" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg/500px-Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4000" data-file-height="2885"></a><figcaption>Trump (rightmost) and his wife Ivana at a 1985 state dinner for King <a href="/wiki/Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia" title="Fahd of Saudi Arabia">Fahd of Saudi Arabia</a> with President <a href="/wiki/Ronald_Reagan" title="Ronald Reagan">Ronald Reagan</a> and First Lady <a href="/wiki/Nancy_Reagan" title="Nancy Reagan">Nancy Reagan</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>In 1977, Trump married <a href="/wiki/Ivana_Zeln%C3%AD%C4%8Dkov%C3%A1" class="mw-redirect" title="Ivana Zelníčková">Ivana Zelníčková</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBlair2015300_662-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlair2015300-662"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>660<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They had three children: <a href="/wiki/Donald_Jr." class="mw-redirect" title="Donald Jr.">Donald Jr.</a> (b. 1977), <a href="/wiki/Ivanka_Trump" title="Ivanka Trump">Ivanka</a> (b. 1981), and <a href="/wiki/Eric_Trump" title="Eric Trump">Eric</a> (b. 1984). Ivana played a crucial role in generating the "fawning press coverage" that shaped Trump's public image as a real estate mogul.<sup id="cite_ref-663" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-663"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>661<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The couple divorced in 1990, following his affair with model and actress <a href="/wiki/Marla_Maples" title="Marla Maples">Marla Maples</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-664" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-664"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>662<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He and Maples married in 1993 and divorced in 1999. They have one daughter, <a href="/wiki/Tiffany_Trump" title="Tiffany Trump">Tiffany</a> (b. 1993), whom Maples raised in California.<sup id="cite_ref-665" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-665"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>663<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 2005, he married Slovenian model <a href="/wiki/Melania_Knauss" class="mw-redirect" title="Melania Knauss">Melania Knauss</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017266_666-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017266-666"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>664<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> They have one son, <a href="/wiki/Barron_Trump" title="Barron Trump">Barron</a> (b. 2006).<sup id="cite_ref-667" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-667"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>665<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Wealth">Wealth</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Wealth_of_Donald_Trump" title="Wealth of Donald Trump">Wealth of Donald Trump</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:1982-_Net_worth_of_Donald_Trump.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/1982-_Net_worth_of_Donald_Trump.svg/330px-1982-_Net_worth_of_Donald_Trump.svg.png" decoding="async" width="330" height="186" style="--mw-file-upright: 1.2" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/1982-_Net_worth_of_Donald_Trump.svg/960px-1982-_Net_worth_of_Donald_Trump.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675"></a><figcaption>Trump's net worth over time, as estimated by <i><a href="/wiki/Forbes" title="Forbes">Forbes</a></i><sup id="cite_ref-Forbes1982-_668-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Forbes1982--668"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>666<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Forbes2001-_669-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Forbes2001--669"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>667<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure><p>
Trump has said he began his career with "a small loan of a million dollars" from his father and that he had to pay it back with interest.<sup id="cite_ref-670" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-670"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>668<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He borrowed at least $60 million from his father, did not repay many of the loans, and received $413 million (2018 equivalent, adjusted for inflation) from his father's real estate company.<sup id="cite_ref-671" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-671"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>669<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-Tax_Schemes_36-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tax_Schemes-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Posing as a Trump Organization official named "<a href="/wiki/John_Barron_(pseudonym)" class="mw-redirect" title="John Barron (pseudonym)">John Barron</a>", Trump called journalist Jonathan Greenberg in 1984, trying to get a higher ranking on the <a href="/wiki/Forbes_400" title="Forbes 400"><i>Forbes</i> 400</a> list of wealthiest Americans.<sup id="cite_ref-672" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-672"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>670<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup></p><div class="paragraphbreak" style="margin-top:0.5em"></div><p>Trump self-reported his net worth over a wide range, from minus $900 million in 1990,<sup id="cite_ref-broke_673-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-broke-673"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>671<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> to $10 billion in 2015.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEJohnston202120_674-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston202120-674"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>672<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> After his business bankruptcies in the 1990s, his lenders forgave more than 80% of his personal debt and allowed him to keep some of his real estate.<sup id="cite_ref-broke_673-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-broke-673"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>671<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 2015, <i><a href="/wiki/Forbes" title="Forbes">Forbes</a></i> estimated his net worth at $4.5 billion, based on interviews with more than 80 sources.<sup id="cite_ref-675" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-675"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>673<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 2026, the magazine set that number at $6.5 billion and ranked him the 645th wealthiest person in the world.<sup id="cite_ref-676" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-676"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>674<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Relationship_with_Jeffrey_Epstein">Relationship with Jeffrey Epstein</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Relationship_of_Donald_Trump_and_Jeffrey_Epstein" title="Relationship of Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein">Relationship of Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein</a></div>
<p>Trump had a 15-year friendship with convicted sex offender <a href="/wiki/Jeffrey_Epstein" title="Jeffrey Epstein">Jeffrey Epstein</a>. Persons who knew them at the time said they frequently "hit on" and competed for women.<sup id="cite_ref-677" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-677"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>675<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Media attention and public pressure mounted in 2025, when his administration did not release <a href="/wiki/Epstein_files" title="Epstein files">files relating to Epstein</a>, despite Trump's promise to do so during the 2024 campaign.<sup id="cite_ref-678" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-678"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>676<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some of the files were released in December 2025, mostly heavily redacted.<sup id="cite_ref-679" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-679"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>677<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Roughly three million additional pages released in 2026 included approximately 38,000 references to Trump, his wife, Mar-a-Lago, and other Trump-related terms. References to Trump included "salacious information" and uncorroborated statements about him from witnesses in transcripts made over several decades.<sup id="cite_ref-680" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-680"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>678<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Health">Health</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Age_and_health_concerns_about_Donald_Trump" title="Age and health concerns about Donald Trump">Age and health concerns about Donald Trump</a></div>
<p>Trump says he has never drunk alcohol, smoked cigarettes, or used drugs,<sup id="cite_ref-681" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-681"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>679<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-682" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-682"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>680<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and that he sleeps about four or five hours a night.<sup id="cite_ref-683" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-683"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>681<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEAlmondDu2020_684-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlmondDu2020-684"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>682<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He has called golfing his "primary form of exercise", but usually does not walk the course.<sup id="cite_ref-685" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-685"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>683<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He considers exercise a waste of energy, believing the body is "like a battery, with a finite amount of energy" which is depleted by exercise.<sup id="cite_ref-686" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-686"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>684<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEO'DonnellRutherford1991133_687-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'DonnellRutherford1991133-687"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>685<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2015, his campaign released a letter from his longtime personal physician, <a href="/wiki/Harold_Bornstein" title="Harold Bornstein">Harold Bornstein</a>, stating that he would "be the healthiest individual ever elected to the presidency".<sup id="cite_ref-dictation_688-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dictation-688"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>686<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 2018, Bornstein said Trump had dictated the contents of the letter and that three of Trump's agents had seized his medical records in a February 2017 raid on Bornstein's office.<sup id="cite_ref-dictation_688-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dictation-688"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>686<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-689" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-689"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>687<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Religion">Religion</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_religion" title="Donald Trump and religion">Donald Trump and religion</a></div>
<p>Trump called himself a <a href="/wiki/Presbyterian" class="mw-redirect" title="Presbyterian">Presbyterian</a> and a <a href="/wiki/Protestant" class="mw-redirect" title="Protestant">Protestant</a> in 2016.<sup id="cite_ref-690" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-690"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>688<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-691" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-691"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>689<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 2020, he said he was a <a href="/wiki/Nondenominational_Christian" class="mw-redirect" title="Nondenominational Christian">nondenominational Christian</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-692" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-692"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>690<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, many have questioned the depth of these religious affiliations. A survey during his first presidency (2017–2021) showed that 63 percent of Americans did not believe that he was religious, despite professing a Christian affiliation, and that only 44 percent believed he was a Christian.<sup id="cite_ref-693" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-693"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>691<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Some of Trump's comments on the Bible or Christian practice have led critical observers to suggest that his knowledge of Christianity is superficial or erroneous, and few biographers have described him as deeply or even particularly religious.<sup id="cite_ref-LATimes17_694-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-LATimes17-694"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>692<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-NPR16_695-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NPR16-695"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>693<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In his first term, Trump appointed his personal pastor<sup id="cite_ref-696" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-696"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>694<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> and spiritual advisor,<sup id="cite_ref-697" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-697"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>695<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> televangelist <a href="/wiki/Paula_White-Cain" title="Paula White-Cain">Paula White-Cain</a>, to the White House <a href="/wiki/Office_of_Public_Liaison" class="mw-redirect" title="Office of Public Liaison">Office of Public Liaison</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-698" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-698"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>696<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> During his second term, he appointed her as senior advisor of the newly created <a href="/wiki/White_House_Faith_Office" class="mw-redirect" title="White House Faith Office">White House Faith Office</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-699" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-699"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>697<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-700" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-700"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>698<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
